title: Hot composting versus warm composting — choosing the right method
subtitle: When the extra management of a hot heap is worth it and when a cold bin is enough
excerpt: A hot compost heap reaches 55 to 70 degrees Celsius in the centre, kills weed seeds and most pathogens, and produces finished compost in 4 to 12 weeks. A warm or cold heap never reaches pathogen-kill temperature and takes 6 to 18 months to mature. The right choice depends on what you are composting and how quickly you need the output.
sourceNotes: WRAP (Waste and Resources Action Programme) composting guidance for householders. Garden Organic (formerly HDRA) composting fact sheets. USDA National Organic Program guidance on pathogen kill temperatures in compost (55°C for 15 days turning 5 times). Eliot Coleman: The Winter Harvest Handbook for compost temperature management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A managed composting method where the heap reaches 55 to 70 degrees Celsius through controlled aeration, moisture, and carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, producing finished compost in weeks rather than months.
The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in compost feedstocks. The optimal range for hot composting is 25:1 to 35:1, balancing energy-rich brown materials with nitrogen-rich green ones.

content.text: requires a minimum pile volume of around 1 m³ to insulate the centre against heat loss, a

of 25:1 to 35:1, adequate moisture (damp like a wrung-out sponge), and turning every 3 to 7 days. When all three conditions are met, the heap heats within 2 to 3 days and kills weed seeds and most pathogens. A warm heap in an insulated bin runs at 25 to 40 degrees Celsius. It relies on slower decomposition and does not reach kill temperatures.
When hot composting is worth the effort

You are composting material with weed seeds: perennial weed roots (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder), lawn clippings with seeding grass, or annual weed heads. A cold bin does not kill seeds; a hot heap does, if the whole pile reaches 55 degrees Celsius for at least 15 days.
You need compost in 8 to 12 weeks: a well-managed hot heap produces finished compost in one season. A cold bin on the same material takes 12 to 18 months.
You have a large volume of mixed material: kitchen scraps, garden waste, paper, and cardboard all heat most effectively in quantity.
When a warm or cold bin is adequate
You compost kitchen scraps and dead-headed plants only, no seeding weeds, no diseased material: a standard dalek bin handles this in a year.

You have a small garden and a small volume of material: a 1 m³ hot pile requires a significant batch of material all at once. If the garden produces a modest trickle of waste, accumulating enough for a hot heap takes months.

A compost thermometer with a probe 200 to 300 mm long reads the centre temperature. Probe in three or four spots at mid-pile height. A heap in good condition shows 55 to 65 degrees Celsius in the centre and 30 to 40 degrees near the edges. When the centre drops below 45 degrees Celsius, the heap has used the available oxygen and needs turning: pull the edges to the centre and the centre to the outside.
